1â†’package kotlinx.coroutines
  2â†’
  3â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.intrinsics.*
  4â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
  5â†’
  6â†’/**
  7â†’ * Defines start options for coroutines builders.
  8â†’ *
  9â†’ * It is used in the `start` parameter of coroutine builder functions like
 10â†’ * [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] and [async][CoroutineScope.async]
 11â†’ * to describe when and how the coroutine should be dispatched initially.
 12â†’ *
 13â†’ * This parameter only affects how the coroutine behaves until the code of its body starts executing.
 14â†’ * After that, cancellability and dispatching are defined by the behavior of the invoked suspending functions.
 15â†’ *
 16â†’ * The summary of coroutine start options is:
 17â†’ * - [DEFAULT] immediately schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context.
 18â†’ * - [LAZY] delays the moment of the initial dispatch until the result of the coroutine is needed.
 19â†’ * - [ATOMIC] prevents the coroutine from being cancelled before it starts, ensuring that its code will start
 20â†’ *   executing in any case.
 21â†’ * - [UNDISPATCHED] immediately executes the coroutine until its first suspension point _in the current thread_.
 22â†’ */
 23â†’public enum class CoroutineStart {
 24â†’    /**
 25â†’     * Immediately schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context. This is usually the default option.
 26â†’     *
 27â†’     * [DEFAULT] uses the default dispatch procedure described in the [CoroutineDispatcher] documentation.
 28â†’     *
 29â†’     * If the coroutine's [Job] is cancelled before it started executing, then it will not start its
 30â†’     * execution at all and will be considered [cancelled][Job.isCancelled].
 31â†’     *
 32â†’     * Examples:
 33â†’     *
 34â†’     * ```
 35â†’     * // Example of starting a new coroutine that goes through a dispatch
 36â†’     * runBlocking {
 37â†’     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")
 38â†’     *     // Dispatch the job to execute later.
 39â†’     *     // The parent coroutine's dispatcher is inherited by default.
 40â†’     *     // In this case, it's the single thread backing `runBlocking`.
 41â†’     *     launch { // CoroutineStart.DEFAULT is launch's default start mode
 42â†’     *         println("3. When the thread is available, we start the coroutine")
 43â†’     *     }
 44â†’     *     println("2. The thread keeps doing other work after launching the coroutine")
 45â†’     * }
 46â†’     * ```
 47â†’     *
 48â†’     * ```
 49â†’     * // Example of starting a new coroutine that doesn't go through a dispatch initially
 50â†’     * runBlocking {
 51â†’     *     println("1. About to start a coroutine not needing a dispatch.")
 52â†’     *     // Dispatch the job to execute.
 53â†’     *     // `Dispatchers.Unconfined` is explicitly chosen.
 54â†’     *     launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // CoroutineStart.DEFAULT is the launch's default start mode
 55â†’     *         println("2. The body will be executed immediately")
 56â†’     *         delay(50.milliseconds) // give up the thread to the outer coroutine
 57â†’     *         println("4. When the thread is next available, this coroutine proceeds further")
 58â†’     *     }
 59â†’     *     println("3. After the initial suspension, the thread does other work.")
 60â†’     * }
 61â†’     * ```
 62â†’     *
 63â†’     * ```
 64â†’     * // Example of cancelling coroutines before they start executing.
 65â†’     * runBlocking {
 66â†’     *     // dispatch the job to execute on this thread later
 67â†’     *     launch { // CoroutineStart.DEFAULT is the launch's default start mode
 68â†’     *         println("This code will never execute")
 69â†’     *     }
 70â†’     *     cancel() // cancels the current coroutine scope and its children
 71â†’     *     launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) {
 72â†’     *         println("This code will never execute")
 73â†’     *     }
 74â†’     *     println("This code will execute.")
 75â†’     * }
 76â†’     * ```
 77â†’     */
 78â†’    DEFAULT,
 79â†’
 80â†’    /**
 81â†’     * Starts the coroutine lazily, only when it is needed.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * Starting a coroutine with [LAZY] only creates the coroutine, but does not schedule it for execution.
 84â†’     * When the completion of the coroutine is first awaited
 85â†’     * (for example, via [Job.join]) or explicitly [started][Job.start],
 86â†’     * the dispatch procedure described in the [CoroutineDispatcher] documentation is performed in the thread
 87â†’     * that did it.
 88â†’     *
 89â†’     * The details of what counts as waiting can be found in the documentation of the corresponding coroutine builders
 90â†’     * like [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] and [async][CoroutineScope.async].
 91â†’     *
 92â†’     * If the coroutine's [Job] is cancelled before it started executing, then it will not start its
 93â†’     * execution at all and will be considered [cancelled][Job.isCancelled].
 94â†’     *
 95â†’     * **Pitfall**: launching a coroutine with [LAZY] without awaiting or cancelling it at any point means that it will
 96â†’     * never be completed, leading to deadlocks and resource leaks.
 97â†’     * For example, the following code will deadlock, since [coroutineScope] waits for all of its child coroutines to
 98â†’     * complete:
 99â†’     * ```
100â†’     * // This code hangs!
101â†’     * coroutineScope {
102â†’     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) { }
103â†’     * }
104â†’     * ```
105â†’     *
106â†’     * The behavior of [LAZY] can be described with the following examples:
107â†’     *
108â†’     * ```
109â†’     * // Example of lazily starting a new coroutine that goes through a dispatch
110â†’     * runBlocking {
111â†’     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")
112â†’     *     // Create a job to execute on `Dispatchers.Default` later.
113â†’     *     val job = launch(Dispatchers.Default, start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) {
114â†’     *         println("3. Only now does the coroutine start.")
115â†’     *     }
116â†’     *     delay(10.milliseconds) // try to give the coroutine some time to run
117â†’     *     println("2. The coroutine still has not started. Now, we join it.")
118â†’     *     job.join()
119â†’     * }
120â†’     * ```
121â†’     *
122â†’     * ```
123â†’     * // Example of lazily starting a new coroutine that doesn't go through a dispatch initially
124â†’     * runBlocking {
125â†’     *     println("1. About to lazily start a new coroutine.")
126â†’     *     // Create a job to execute on `Dispatchers.Unconfined` later.
127â†’     *     val lazyJob = launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined, start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) {
128â†’     *         println("3. The coroutine starts on the thread that called `join`.")
129â†’     *     }
130â†’     *     // We start the job on another thread for illustrative purposes
131â†’     *     launch(Dispatchers.Default) {
132â†’     *         println("2. We start the lazyJob.")
133â†’     *         job.start() // runs lazyJob's code in-place
134â†’     *         println("4. Only now does the `start` call return.")
135â†’     *     }
136â†’     * }
137â†’     * ```
138â†’     *
139â†’     * ## Alternatives
140â†’     *
141â†’     * The effects of [LAZY] can usually be achieved more idiomatically without it.
142â†’     *
143â†’     * When a coroutine is started with [LAZY] and is stored in a property,
144â†’     * it may be a better choice to use [lazy] instead:
145â†’     *
146â†’     * ```
147â†’     * // instead of `val page = scope.async(start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) { getPage() }`, do
148â†’     * val page by lazy { scope.async { getPage() } }
149â†’     * ```
150â†’     *
151â†’     * This way, the child coroutine is not created at all unless it is needed.
152â†’     * Note that with this, any access to this variable will start the coroutine,
153â†’     * even something like `page.invokeOnCompletion { }` or `page.isActive`.
154â†’     *
155â†’     * If a coroutine is started with [LAZY] and then unconditionally started,
156â†’     * it is more idiomatic to create the coroutine in the exact place where it is started:
157â†’     *
158â†’     * ```
159â†’     * // instead of `val job = scope.launch(start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) { }; job.start()`, do
160â†’     * scope.launch { }
161â†’     * ```
162â†’     */
163â†’    LAZY,
164â†’
165â†’    /**
166â†’     * Atomically (i.e., in a non-cancellable way) schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context.
167â†’     *
168â†’     * This is similar to [DEFAULT], but the coroutine is guaranteed to start executing even if it was cancelled.
169â†’     * This only affects the behavior until the body of the coroutine starts executing;
170â†’     * inside the body, cancellation will work as usual.
171â†’     *
172â†’     * Like [ATOMIC], [UNDISPATCHED], too, ensures that coroutines will be started in any case.
173â†’     * The difference is that, instead of immediately starting them on the same thread,
174â†’     * [ATOMIC] performs the full dispatch procedure just as [DEFAULT] does.
175â†’     *
176â†’     * Because of this, we can use [ATOMIC] in cases where we want to be certain that some code eventually runs
177â†’     * and uses a specific dispatcher to do that.
178â†’     *
179â†’     * Example:
180â†’     * ```
181â†’     * val mutex = Mutex()
182â†’     *
183â†’     * mutex.lock() // lock the mutex outside the coroutine
184â†’     * // ... // initial portion of the work, protected by the mutex
185â†’     * val job = launch(start = CoroutineStart.ATOMIC) {
186â†’     *     // the work must continue in a coroutine, but still under the mutex
187â†’     *     println("Coroutine running!")
188â†’     *     try {
189â†’     *         // this `try` block will be entered in any case because of ATOMIC
190â†’     *         println("Starting task...")
191â†’     *         delay(10.milliseconds) // throws due to cancellation
192â†’     *         println("Finished task.")
193â†’     *     } finally {
194â†’     *         mutex.unlock() // correctly release the mutex
195â†’     *     }
196â†’     * }
197â†’     *
198â†’     * job.cancelAndJoin() // we immediately cancel the coroutine.
199â†’     * mutex.withLock {
200â†’     *     println("The lock has been returned correctly!")
201â†’     * }
202â†’     * ```
203â†’     *
204â†’     * Here, we used [ATOMIC] to ensure that a mutex that was acquired outside the coroutine does get released
205â†’     * even if cancellation happens between `lock()` and `launch`.
206â†’     * As a result, the mutex will always be released.
207â†’     *
208â†’     * The behavior of [ATOMIC] can be described with the following examples:
209â†’     *
210â†’     * ```
211â†’     * // Example of cancelling atomically started coroutines
212â†’     * runBlocking {
213â†’     *     println("1. Atomically starting a coroutine that goes through a dispatch.")
214â†’     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.ATOMIC) {
215â†’     *         check(!isActive) // attempting to suspend later will throw
216â†’     *         println("4. The coroutine was cancelled (isActive = $isActive), but starts anyway.")
217â†’     *         try {
218â†’     *             delay(10.milliseconds) // will throw: the coroutine is cancelled
219â†’     *             println("This code will never run.")
220â†’     *         } catch (e: CancellationException) {
221â†’     *             println("5. Cancellation at later points still works.")
222â†’     *             throw e
223â†’     *         }
224â†’     *     }
225â†’     *     println("2. Cancelling this coroutine and all of its children.")
226â†’     *     cancel()
227â†’     *     launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined, start = CoroutineStart.ATOMIC) {
228â†’     *         check(!isActive) // attempting to suspend will throw
229â†’     *         println("3. An undispatched coroutine starts.")
230â†’     *     }
231â†’     *     ensureActive() // we can even crash the current coroutine.
232â†’     * }
233â†’     * ```
234â†’     *
235â†’     * This is a **delicate** API. The coroutine starts execution even if its [Job] is cancelled before starting.
236â†’     * However, the resources used within a coroutine may rely on the cancellation mechanism,
237â†’     * and cannot be used after the [Job] cancellation. For instance, in Android development, updating a UI element
238â†’     * is not allowed if the coroutine's scope, which is tied to the element's lifecycle, has been cancelled.
239â†’     */
240â†’    @DelicateCoroutinesApi
241â†’    ATOMIC,
242â†’
243â†’    /**
244â†’     * Immediately executes the coroutine until its first suspension point _in the current thread_.
245â†’     *
246â†’     * Starting a coroutine using [UNDISPATCHED] is similar to using [Dispatchers.Unconfined] with [DEFAULT], except:
247â†’     * - Resumptions from later suspensions will properly use the actual dispatcher from the coroutine's context.
248â†’     *   Only the code until the first suspension point will be executed immediately.
249â†’     * - Even if the coroutine was cancelled already, its code will still start running, similar to [ATOMIC].
250â†’     * - The coroutine will not form an event loop. See [Dispatchers.Unconfined] for an explanation of event loops.
251â†’     *
252â†’     * This set of behaviors makes [UNDISPATCHED] well-suited for cases where the coroutine has a distinct
253â†’     * initialization phase whose side effects we want to rely on later.
254â†’     *
255â†’     * Example:
256â†’     * ```
257â†’     * var tasks = 0
258â†’     * repeat(3) {
259â†’     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
260â†’     *         tasks++
261â†’     *         try {
262â†’     *             println("Waiting for a reply...")
263â†’     *             delay(50.milliseconds)
264â†’     *             println("Got a reply!")
265â†’     *         } finally {
266â†’     *             tasks--
267â†’     *         }
268â†’     *     }
269â†’     * }
270â†’     * // Because of UNDISPATCHED,
271â†’     * // we know that the tasks already ran to their first suspension point,
272â†’     * // so this number is non-zero initially.
273â†’     * while (tasks > 0) {
274â†’     *     println("currently active: $tasks")
275â†’     *     delay(10.milliseconds)
276â†’     * }
277â†’     * ```
278â†’     *
279â†’     * Here, we implement a publisher-subscriber interaction, where [UNDISPATCHED] ensures that the
280â†’     * subscribers do get registered before the publisher first checks if it can stop emitting values due to
281â†’     * the lack of subscribers.
282â†’     *
283â†’     * ```
284â†’     * // Constant usage of stack space
285â†’     * fun CoroutineScope.factorialWithUnconfined(n: Int): Deferred<Int> =
286â†’     *     async(Dispatchers.Unconfined) {
287â†’     *         if (n > 0) {
288â†’     *             n * factorialWithUnconfined(n - 1).await()
289â†’     *         } else {
290â†’     *             1 // replace with `error()` to see the stacktrace
291â†’     *         }
292â†’     *     }
293â†’     *
294â†’     * // Linearly increasing usage of stack space
295â†’     * fun CoroutineScope.factorialWithUndispatched(n: Int): Deferred<Int> =
296â†’     *     async(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
297â†’     *         if (n > 0) {
298â†’     *             n * factorialWithUndispatched(n - 1).await()
299â†’     *         } else {
300â†’     *             1 // replace with `error()` to see the stacktrace
301â†’     *         }
302â†’     *     }
303â†’     * ```
304â†’     *
305â†’     * Calling `factorialWithUnconfined` from this example will result in a constant-size stack,
306â†’     * whereas `factorialWithUndispatched` will lead to `n` recursively nested calls,
307â†’     * resulting in a stack overflow for large values of `n`.
308â†’     *
309â†’     * The behavior of [UNDISPATCHED] can be described with the following examples:
310â†’     *
311â†’     * ```
312â†’     * runBlocking {
313â†’     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")
314â†’     *     launch(Dispatchers.Default, start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
315â†’     *         println("2. The coroutine is immediately started in the same thread.")
316â†’     *         delay(10.milliseconds)
317â†’     *         println("4. The execution continues in a Dispatchers.Default thread.")
318â†’     *     }
319â†’     *     println("3. Execution of the outer coroutine only continues later.")
320â†’     * }
321â†’     * ```
322â†’     *
323â†’     * ```
324â†’     * // Cancellation does not prevent the coroutine from being started
325â†’     * runBlocking {
326â†’     *     println("1. First, we cancel this scope.")
327â†’     *     cancel()
328â†’     *     println("2. Now, we start a new UNDISPATCHED child.")
329â†’     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
330â†’     *         check(!isActive) // the child is already cancelled
331â†’     *         println("3. We entered the coroutine despite being cancelled.")
332â†’     *     }
333â†’     *     println("4. Execution of the outer coroutine only continues later.")
334â†’     * }
335â†’     * ```
336â†’     *
337â†’     * **Pitfall**: unlike [Dispatchers.Unconfined] and [MainCoroutineDispatcher.immediate], nested undispatched
338â†’     * coroutines do not form an event loop that otherwise prevents potential stack overflow in case of unlimited
339â†’     * nesting. This property is necessary for the use case of guaranteed initialization, but may be undesirable in
340â†’     * other cases.
341â†’     * See [Dispatchers.Unconfined] for an explanation of event loops.
342â†’     */
343â†’    UNDISPATCHED;
344â†’
345â†’    /**
346â†’     * Starts the corresponding block with receiver as a coroutine with this coroutine start strategy.
347â†’     *
348â†’     * - [DEFAULT] uses [startCoroutineCancellable].
349â†’     * - [ATOMIC] uses [startCoroutine].
350â†’     * - [UNDISPATCHED] uses [startCoroutineUndispatched].
351â†’     * - [LAZY] does nothing.
352â†’     *
353â†’     * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
354â†’     */
355â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
356â†’    public operator fun <R, T> invoke(block: suspend R.() -> T, receiver: R, completion: Continuation<T>): Unit =
357â†’        when (this) {
358â†’            DEFAULT -> block.startCoroutineCancellable(receiver, completion)
359â†’            ATOMIC -> block.startCoroutine(receiver, completion)
360â†’            UNDISPATCHED -> block.startCoroutineUndispatched(receiver, completion)
361â†’            LAZY -> Unit // will start lazily
362â†’        }
363â†’
364â†’    /**
365â†’     * Returns `true` when [LAZY].
366â†’     *
367â†’     * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
368â†’     */
369â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
370â†’    public val isLazy: Boolean get() = this === LAZY

{"file_path": "/project/kotlinx-coroutines-core/common/src/CoroutineStart.kt", "content": "package kotlinx.coroutines

import kotlinx.coroutines.intrinsics.*
import kotlin.coroutines.*

/**
 * Defines start options for coroutines builders.
 *
 * It is used in the `start` parameter of coroutine builder functions like
 * [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] and [async][CoroutineScope.async]
 * to describe when and how the coroutine should be dispatched initially.
 *
 * This parameter only affects how the coroutine behaves until the code of its body starts executing.
 * After that, cancellability and dispatching are defined by the behavior of the invoked suspending functions.
 *
 * The summary of coroutine start options is:
 * - [DEFAULT] immediately schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context.
 * - [LAZY] delays the moment of the initial dispatch until the result of the coroutine is needed.
 * - [ATOMIC] prevents the coroutine from being cancelled before it starts, ensuring that its code will start
 *   executing in any case.
 * - [UNDISPATCHED] immediately executes the coroutine until its first suspension point _in the current thread_.
 */
public enum class CoroutineStart {
    /**
     * Immediately schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context. This is usually the default option.
     *
     * [DEFAULT] uses the default dispatch procedure described in the [CoroutineDispatcher] documentation.
     *
     * If the coroutine's [Job] is cancelled before it started executing, then it will not start its
     * execution at all and will be considered [cancelled][Job.isCancelled].
     *
     * Examples:
     *
     * ```
     * // Example of starting a new coroutine that goes through a dispatch
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")
     *     // Dispatch the job to execute later.
     *     // The parent coroutine's dispatcher is inherited by default.
     *     // In this case, it's the single thread backing `runBlocking`.
     *     launch { // CoroutineStart.DEFAULT is launch's default start mode
     *         println("3. When the thread is available, we start the coroutine")
     *     }
     *     println("2. The thread keeps doing other work after launching the coroutine")
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * ```
     * // Example of starting a new coroutine that doesn't go through a dispatch initially
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. About to start a coroutine not needing a dispatch.")
     *     // Dispatch the job to execute.
     *     // `Dispatchers.Unconfined` is explicitly chosen.
     *     launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // CoroutineStart.DEFAULT is the launch's default start mode
     *         println("2. The body will be executed immediately")
     *         delay(50.milliseconds) // give up the thread to the outer coroutine
     *         println("4. When the thread is next available, this coroutine proceeds further")
     *     }
     *     println("3. After the initial suspension, the thread does other work.")
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * ```
     * // Example of cancelling coroutines before they start executing.
     * runBlocking {
     *     // dispatch the job to execute on this thread later
     *     launch { // CoroutineStart.DEFAULT is the launch's default start mode
     *         println("This code will never execute")
     *     }
     *     cancel() // cancels the current coroutine scope and its children
     *     launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) {
     *         println("This code will never execute")
     *     }
     *     println("This code will execute.")
     * }
     * ```
     */
    DEFAULT,

    /**
     * Starts the coroutine lazily, only when it is needed.
     *
     * Starting a coroutine with [LAZY] only creates the coroutine, but does not schedule it for execution.
     * When the completion of the coroutine is first awaited
     * (for example, via [Job.join]) or explicitly [started][Job.start],
     * the dispatch procedure described in the [CoroutineDispatcher] documentation is performed in the thread
     * that did it.
     *
     * The details of what counts as waiting can be found in the documentation of the corresponding coroutine builders
     * like [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] and [async][CoroutineScope.async].
     *
     * If the coroutine's [Job] is cancelled before it started executing, then it will not start its
     * execution at all and will be considered [cancelled][Job.isCancelled].
     *
     * **Pitfall**: launching a coroutine with [LAZY] without awaiting or cancelling it at any point means that it will
     * never be completed, leading to deadlocks and resource leaks.
     * For example, the following code will deadlock, since [coroutineScope] waits for all of its child coroutines to
     * complete:
     * ```
     * // This code hangs!
     * coroutineScope {
     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) { }
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * The behavior of [LAZY] can be described with the following examples:
     *
     * ```
     * // Example of lazily starting a new coroutine that goes through a dispatch
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")
     *     // Create a job to execute on `Dispatchers.Default` later.
     *     val job = launch(Dispatchers.Default, start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) {
     *         println("3. Only now does the coroutine start.")
     *     }
     *     delay(10.milliseconds) // try to give the coroutine some time to run
     *     println("2. The coroutine still has not started. Now, we join it.")
     *     job.join()
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * ```
     * // Example of lazily starting a new coroutine that doesn't go through a dispatch initially
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. About to lazily start a new coroutine.")
     *     // Create a job to execute on `Dispatchers.Unconfined` later.
     *     val lazyJob = launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined, start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) {
     *         println("3. The coroutine starts on the thread that called `join`.")
     *     }
     *     // We start the job on another thread for illustrative purposes
     *     launch(Dispatchers.Default) {
     *         println("2. We start the lazyJob.")
     *         job.start() // runs lazyJob's code in-place
     *         println("4. Only now does the `start` call return.")
     *     }
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * ## Alternatives
     *
     * The effects of [LAZY] can usually be achieved more idiomatically without it.
     *
     * When a coroutine is started with [LAZY] and is stored in a property,
     * it may be a better choice to use [lazy] instead:
     *
     * ```
     * // instead of `val page = scope.async(start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) { getPage() }`, do
     * val page by lazy { scope.async { getPage() } }
     * ```
     *
     * This way, the child coroutine is not created at all unless it is needed.
     * Note that with this, any access to this variable will start the coroutine,
     * even something like `page.invokeOnCompletion { }` or `page.isActive`.
     *
     * If a coroutine is started with [LAZY] and then unconditionally started,
     * it is more idiomatic to create the coroutine in the exact place where it is started:
     *
     * ```
     * // instead of `val job = scope.launch(start = CoroutineStart.LAZY) { }; job.start()`, do
     * scope.launch { }
     * ```
     */
    LAZY,

    /**
     * Atomically (i.e., in a non-cancellable way) schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context.
     *
     * This is similar to [DEFAULT], but the coroutine is guaranteed to start executing even if it was cancelled.
     * This only affects the behavior until the body of the coroutine starts executing;
     * inside the body, cancellation will work as usual.
     *
     * Like [ATOMIC], [UNDISPATCHED], too, ensures that coroutines will be started in any case.
     * The difference is that, instead of immediately starting them on the same thread,
     * [ATOMIC] performs the full dispatch procedure just as [DEFAULT] does.
     *
     * Because of this, we can use [ATOMIC] in cases where we want to be certain that some code eventually runs
     * and uses a specific dispatcher to do that.
     *
     * Example:
     * ```
     * val mutex = Mutex()
     *
     * mutex.lock() // lock the mutex outside the coroutine
     * // ... // initial portion of the work, protected by the mutex
     * val job = launch(start = CoroutineStart.ATOMIC) {
     *     // the work must continue in a coroutine, but still under the mutex
     *     println("Coroutine running!")
     *     try {
     *         // this `try` block will be entered in any case because of ATOMIC
     *         println("Starting task...")
     *         delay(10.milliseconds) // throws due to cancellation
     *         println("Finished task.")
     *     } finally {
     *         mutex.unlock() // correctly release the mutex
     *     }
     * }
     *
     * job.cancelAndJoin() // we immediately cancel the coroutine.
     * mutex.withLock {
     *     println("The lock has been returned correctly!")
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * Here, we used [ATOMIC] to ensure that a mutex that was acquired outside the coroutine does get released
     * even if cancellation happens between `lock()` and `launch`.
     * As a result, the mutex will always be released.
     *
     * The behavior of [ATOMIC] can be described with the following examples:
     *
     * ```
     * // Example of cancelling atomically started coroutines
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. Atomically starting a coroutine that goes through a dispatch.")
     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.ATOMIC) {
     *         check(!isActive) // attempting to suspend later will throw
     *         println("4. The coroutine was cancelled (isActive = $isActive), but starts anyway.")
     *         try {
     *             delay(10.milliseconds) // will throw: the coroutine is cancelled
     *             println("This code will never run.")
     *         } catch (e: CancellationException) {
     *             println("5. Cancellation at later points still works.")
     *             throw e
     *         }
     *     }
     *     println("2. Cancelling this coroutine and all of its children.")
     *     cancel()
     *     launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined, start = CoroutineStart.ATOMIC) {
     *         check(!isActive) // attempting to suspend will throw
     *         println("3. An undispatched coroutine starts.")
     *     }
     *     ensureActive() // we can even crash the current coroutine.
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * This is a **delicate** API. The coroutine starts execution even if its [Job] is cancelled before starting.
     * However, the resources used within a coroutine may rely on the cancellation mechanism,
     * and cannot be used after the [Job] cancellation. For instance, in Android development, updating a UI element
     * is not allowed if the coroutine's scope, which is tied to the element's lifecycle, has been cancelled.
     */
    @DelicateCoroutinesApi
    ATOMIC,

    /**
     * Immediately executes the coroutine until its first suspension point _in the current thread_.
     *
     * Starting a coroutine using [UNDISPATCHED] is similar to using [Dispatchers.Unconfined] with [DEFAULT], except:
     * - Resumptions from later suspensions will properly use the actual dispatcher from the coroutine's context.
     *   Only the code until the first suspension point will be executed immediately.
     * - Even if the coroutine was cancelled already, its code will still start running, similar to [ATOMIC].
     * - The coroutine will not form an event loop. See [Dispatchers.Unconfined] for an explanation of event loops.
     *
     * This set of behaviors makes [UNDISPATCHED] well-suited for cases where the coroutine has a distinct
     * initialization phase whose side effects we want to rely on later.
     *
     * Example:
     * ```
     * var tasks = 0
     * repeat(3) {
     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
     *         tasks++
     *         try {
     *             println("Waiting for a reply...")
     *             delay(50.milliseconds)
     *             println("Got a reply!")
     *         } finally {
     *             tasks--
     *         }
     *     }
     * }
     * // Because of UNDISPATCHED,
     * // we know that the tasks already ran to their first suspension point,
     * // so this number is non-zero initially.
     * while (tasks > 0) {
     *     println("currently active: $tasks")
     *     delay(10.milliseconds)
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * Here, we implement a publisher-subscriber interaction, where [UNDISPATCHED] ensures that the
     * subscribers do get registered before the publisher first checks if it can stop emitting values due to
     * the lack of subscribers.
     *
     * ```
     * // Constant usage of stack space
     * fun CoroutineScope.factorialWithUnconfined(n: Int): Deferred<Int> =
     *     async(Dispatchers.Unconfined) {
     *         if (n > 0) {
     *             n * factorialWithUnconfined(n - 1).await()
     *         } else {
     *             1 // replace with `error()` to see the stacktrace
     *         }
     *     }
     *
     * // Linearly increasing usage of stack space
     * fun CoroutineScope.factorialWithUndispatched(n: Int): Deferred<Int> =
     *     async(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
     *         if (n > 0) {
     *             n * factorialWithUndispatched(n - 1).await()
     *         } else {
     *             1 // replace with `error()` to see the stacktrace
     *         }
     *     }
     * ```
     *
     * Calling `factorialWithUnconfined` from this example will result in a constant-size stack,
     * whereas `factorialWithUndispatched` will lead to `n` recursively nested calls,
     * resulting in a stack overflow for large values of `n`.
     *
     * The behavior of [UNDISPATCHED] can be described with the following examples:
     *
     * ```
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")
     *     launch(Dispatchers.Default, start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
     *         println("2. The coroutine is immediately started in the same thread.")
     *         delay(10.milliseconds)
     *         println("4. The execution continues in a Dispatchers.Default thread.")
     *     }
     *     println("3. Execution of the outer coroutine only continues later.")
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * ```
     * // Cancellation does not prevent the coroutine from being started
     * runBlocking {
     *     println("1. First, we cancel this scope.")
     *     cancel()
     *     println("2. Now, we start a new UNDISPATCHED child.")
     *     launch(start = CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED) {
     *         check(!isActive) // the child is already cancelled
     *         println("3. We entered the coroutine despite being cancelled.")
     *     }
     *     println("4. Execution of the outer coroutine only continues later.")
     * }
     * ```
     *
     * **Pitfall**: unlike [Dispatchers.Unconfined] and [MainCoroutineDispatcher.immediate], nested undispatched
     * coroutines do not form an event loop that otherwise prevents potential stack overflow in case of unlimited
     * nesting. This property is necessary for the use case of guaranteed initialization, but may be undesirable in
     * other cases.
     * See [Dispatchers.Unconfined] for an explanation of event loops.
     */
    UNDISPATCHED;

    /**
     * Starts the corresponding block with receiver as a coroutine with this coroutine start strategy.
     *
     * - [DEFAULT] uses [startCoroutineCancellable].
     * - [ATOMIC] uses [startCoroutineAtomic].
     * - [UNDISPATCHED] uses [startCoroutineUndispatched].
     * - [LAZY] does nothing.
     *
     * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
     */
    @InternalCoroutinesApi
    public operator fun <R, T> invoke(block: suspend R.() -> T, receiver: R, completion: Continuation<T>): Unit =
        when (this) {
            DEFAULT -> block.startCoroutineCancellable(receiver, completion)
            ATOMIC -> block.startCoroutineAtomic(receiver, completion)
            UNDISPATCHED -> block.startCoroutineUndispatched(receiver, completion)
            LAZY -> Unit // will start lazily
        }

    /**
     * Returns `true` when [LAZY].
     *
     * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
     */
    @InternalCoroutinesApi
    public val isLazy: Boolean get() = this === LAZY
}
"}

Good. Now let me also check if there's a special handling needed in `undispatchedResult`. Let me think about what happens with the fix.

With the fix, when `CoroutineStart.ATOMIC` fails to dispatch:
1. `startCoroutineAtomic` catches the `DispatchException`
2. `dispatcherFailure` resumes the `ProducerCoroutine` completion with the unwrapped exception
3. The `ProducerCoroutine` transitions from active to completed-exceptionally
4. `dispatcherFailure` rethrows the unwrapped exception
5. This propagates back through `produce` â†’ `produceImpl` â†’ the block inside `coroutineScope`
6. In `undispatchedResult`, the exception is caught and wrapped in `CompletedExceptionally`
7. `makeCompletingOnce` is called on the `ScopeCoroutine`
8. The `ScopeCoroutine` cancels its children - but the `ProducerCoroutine` is already completed
9. `tryWaitForChild` finds the child is already done, so no waiting
10. The exception propagates up properly

Wait, but there's a subtlety. In step 2, `completion.resumeWith(Result.failure(reportException))` is called. Since `ProducerCoroutine` extends `AbstractCoroutine`, its `resumeWith` will call `makeCompletingOnce`. This should work - it'll transition the `ProducerCoroutine` from active to completed-exceptionally.

But hold on - after `resumeWith`, `ProducerCoroutine.afterResume`/`afterCompletion` will be called, which will close the channel and potentially call `handleCoroutineException`. Let me think about this...

Actually, `ProducerCoroutine.onCancelled` will be called:
```kotlin
override fun onCancelled(cause: Throwable, handled: Boolean) {
    val processed = _channel.close(cause)
    if (!processed && !handled) handleCoroutineException(context, cause)
}
```

The `handled` flag depends on whether the parent handled the exception. Since the `ProducerCoroutine` is a child of the `ScopeCoroutine` (which is an instance of `ScopeCoroutine`, whose `isScopedCoroutine` returns `true`), the exception should be handled.

Now let me also think about `dispatcherFailure` rethrowing. In step 4, the rethrown exception is the **unwrapped** exception (not `DispatchException`). This propagates through `produce` â†’ `produceImpl` â†’ the block in `coroutineScope`.

In `undispatchedResult`, the catch block catches this unwrapped exception:
```kotlin
val result = try {
    startBlock()
} catch (e: Throwable) {
    CompletedExceptionally(e)
}
```

Then `makeCompletingOnce(CompletedExceptionally(unwrappedException))` is called on the `ScopeCoroutine`. This should work fine since the `ProducerCoroutine` child is already completed.

```kotlin
} catch (e: DispatchException) {
    handleCoroutineException(delegate.context, e.cause)
}
```

So `DispatchException` from `DispatchedTask.run()` is caught and handled. Good.

For `DispatchedContinuation.resumeWith` (which is called for `ATOMIC` mode), the `DispatchException` is thrown from `safeDispatch`:
```kotlin
override fun resumeWith(result: Result<T>) {
    val state = result.toState()
    if (dispatcher.safeIsDispatchNeeded(context)) {
        _state = state
        resumeMode = MODE_ATOMIC
        dispatcher.safeDispatch(context, this)
    } else {
        executeUnconfined(state, MODE_ATOMIC) {
            withCoroutineContext(context, countOrElement) {
                continuation.resumeWith(result)
            }
        }
    }
}
```

If `safeIsDispatchNeeded` throws `DispatchException` or `safeDispatch` throws `DispatchException`, it propagates out of `resumeWith`. For the `ATOMIC` start path, this is called from `startCoroutine` â†’ `intercepted().resumeWith(Result.success(Unit))`.

With my fix, `startCoroutineAtomic` catches this via `runSafely`, completes the coroutine, and rethrows. 

Now let me also think about edge cases:

1. What if the `DispatchException` is thrown from `safeIsDispatchNeeded` rather than `safeDispatch`?
   - Same thing - it propagates out of `resumeWith`, caught by `runSafely`.

2. What if the coroutine was already cancelled before dispatch failed?
   - The `ProducerCoroutine` is started with `ATOMIC`, so it's guaranteed to start. But if it was cancelled before dispatch, the dispatch still happens. If dispatch fails, the coroutine still needs to be completed.
   - With my fix, `completion.resumeWith(Result.failure(exception))` is called. If the coroutine is already in a cancelling state, `makeCompletingOnce` will handle it correctly.

3. What about the `startCoroutineUndispatched` path for `UNDISPATCHED` mode?
   - This is already handled correctly - it catches `DispatchException` and resumes the completion.

content.text:
Now in `undispatchedResult`, with my fix, the `DispatchException` from the `ATOMIC` start path is already handled:
1. `dispatcherFailure` completes the `ProducerCoroutine` (with unwrapped exception)
2. `dispatcherFailure` rethrows the unwrapped exception (not `DispatchException`)
3. The unwrapped exception is caught in `undispatchedResult` and wrapped in `CompletedExceptionally`
4. `makeCompletingOnce` succeeds quickly (child is already complete)
5. The exception propagates up correctly

But what about the `startCoroutineUndispatched` function? It unwraps `DispatchException`:
```kotlin
val reportException = if (e is DispatchException) e.cause else e
```

Since `dispatcherFailure` rethrows the **unwrapped** exception, this unwrapping check is no longer necessary for the `ATOMIC` path (it won't encounter `DispatchException`). But the `startCoroutineUndispatched` function handles its own `DispatchException`s (from the body's suspension), so it's still correct.

Wait, but what about `undispatchedResult`? It catches the exception directly:
```kotlin
val result = try {
    startBlock()
} catch (e: Throwable) {
    CompletedExceptionally(e)
}
```

The caught exception `e` is the unwrapped one (since `dispatcherFailure` rethrows the unwrapped version). So `CompletedExceptionally(e)` wraps the actual cause, not the `DispatchException`. This is correct.

But wait, what if `dispatcherFailure` throws `DispatchException` directly (not unwrapped)? Looking at the code:
```kotlin
private fun dispatcherFailure(completion: Continuation<*>, e: Throwable) {
    val reportException = if (e is DispatchException) e.cause else e
    completion.resumeWith(Result.failure(reportException))
    throw reportException
}
```

`throw reportException` throws the unwrapped exception, so `undispatchedResult` never sees `DispatchException`. Good.

Now let me also make sure I haven't broken the `UNDISPATCHED` path in `undispatchedResult`. In `undispatchedResult`, the `startBlock()` is `block.startCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn(receiver, this)`. When the block body suspends and the continuation's dispatch fails with `DispatchException`, the exception propagates through `startCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn`. The current code wraps it in `CompletedExceptionally(DispatchException(...))`.

Hmm, actually this is a separate case. In `undispatchedResult`, when the block suspends and then the dispatched continuation fails, the `DispatchException` might propagate to `startCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn`. Let me think...

Actually, when the block suspends, `startCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn` returns `COROUTINE_SUSPENDED`. The dispatch failure would happen asynchronously later, not within `startCoroutineUninterceptedOrReturn`. So `DispatchException` can't reach `undispatchedResult` this way.

The only way `DispatchException` reaches `undispatchedResult.startBlock()` is if it's thrown synchronously within the block body (e.g., from creating and starting a child coroutine with a failing dispatcher), which is exactly the `flowOn` case.

With my fix, the `DispatchException` from child coroutine start is caught by `runSafely` â†’ `dispatcherFailure`, which completes the child and rethrows the unwrapped exception. So `undispatchedResult` sees the unwrapped exception, not `DispatchException`. This is the correct behavior.

Now, let me think about one more edge case. What if `completion.resumeWith(Result.failure(reportException))` in `dispatcherFailure` throws an exception? This could happen if the completion's `resumeWith` triggers some side effect that throws.

Looking at `AbstractCoroutine.resumeWith`:
```kotlin
public final override fun resumeWith(result: Result<T>) {
    val state = makeCompletingOnce(result.toState())
    if (state === COMPLETING_WAITING_CHILDREN) return
    afterResume(state)
}
```

`makeCompletingOnce` might throw `IllegalStateException` if the coroutine is already completing. But in our case, the `ProducerCoroutine` was just created and started (but dispatch failed), so it should be in its initial active state. The `resumeWith` should succeed.

But what if somehow the `ProducerCoroutine` is already in completing state (e.g., due to parent cancellation happening concurrently)? In that case, `makeCompletingOnce` would throw `IllegalStateException`. But since we're in the same thread (the dispatch failure happens synchronously), there shouldn't be concurrent modifications.

I think the fix is correct. Let me also verify by looking at the actual test in `FailFastOnStartTest` for `produce`:

```kotlin
@Test
fun testProduce() = runTest(expected = ::mainException) {
    produce<Int>(Dispatchers.Main) { fail() }
}
```

This test creates a `produce` with `Dispatchers.Main` (which throws). With `DEFAULT` start mode, `runSafely`/`dispatcherFailure` already handles this case (completes the coroutine, rethrows). The test expects `mainException` to be thrown.

The `flowOn` case is different because:
1. `flowOn` creates a `ChannelFlowOperatorImpl` which uses `produceImpl` with `CoroutineStart.ATOMIC`
2. The `produce` call inside `produceImpl` fails during dispatch

With my fix, the `ATOMIC` start path now uses `startCoroutineAtomic` which has the same `runSafely`/`dispatcherFailure` handling. So the `ProducerCoroutine` gets properly completed, the exception propagates, and the flow doesn't hang.

33â†’internal fun Continuation<Unit>.startCoroutineCancellable(fatalCompletion: Continuation<*>) =
34â†’    runSafely(fatalCompletion) {

36â†’    }
37â†’
38â†’/**
39â†’ * Use this function to start a coroutine atomically (non-cancellably) and safely handle dispatcher failures.
40â†’ * If the dispatcher throws during the initial dispatch, the coroutine is completed with the exception
41â†’ * so that it won't prevent its parent from completion.
42â†’ */
43â†’internal fun <R, T> (suspend (R) -> T).startCoroutineAtomic(
44â†’    receiver: R, completion: Continuation<T>,
45â†’) = runSafely(completion) {
46â†’    startCoroutine(receiver, completion)
47â†’}
48â†’
49â†’/**
50â†’ * Runs given block and completes completion with its exception if it occurs.
51â†’ * Rationale: [startCoroutineCancellable] is invoked when we are about to run coroutine asynchronously in its own dispatcher.
52â†’ * Thus if dispatcher throws an exception during coroutine start, coroutine never completes, so we should treat dispatcher exception
53â†’ * as its cause and resume completion.
54â†’ */
55â†’private inline fun runSafely(completion: Continuation<*>, block: () -> Unit) {
56â†’    try {
57â†’        block()
58â†’    } catch (e: Throwable) {
59â†’        dispatcherFailure(completion, e)
60â†’    }
61â†’}
62â†’
63â†’private fun dispatcherFailure(completion: Continuation<*>, e: Throwable) {
64â†’    /*
65â†’     * This method is invoked when we failed to start a coroutine due to the throwing
66â†’     * dispatcher implementation or missing Dispatchers.Main.
67â†’     * This situation is not recoverable, so we are trying to deliver the exception by all means:
68â†’     * 1) Resume the coroutine with an exception, so it won't prevent its parent from completion
69â†’     * 2) Rethrow the exception immediately, so it will crash the caller (e.g. when the coroutine had
70â†’     *    no parent or it was async/produce over MainScope).
71â†’     */
72â†’    val reportException = if (e is DispatchException) e.cause else e
73â†’    completion.resumeWith(Result.failure(reportException))
74â†’    throw reportException
75â†’}

content.text:
The changes look correct. Let me provide a summary of the fix:

## Summary

### Root Cause
When `flowOn` is used with a dispatcher that throws during dispatch (e.g., `Dispatchers.Main.immediate` not present in classpath), the flow collection hangs silently.

The hang occurs because:
1. `flowOn` internally creates a `ChannelFlowOperator` that uses `produce` with `CoroutineStart.ATOMIC` to create a `ProducerCoroutine`
2. When the dispatcher throws during the initial dispatch, a `DispatchException` is thrown
3. The `ProducerCoroutine` was already registered as a child of the parent `ScopeCoroutine` (from `coroutineScope`), but its body never executes
4. The parent catches the exception and tries to complete, but has to wait for the child `ProducerCoroutine` to finish
5. The `ProducerCoroutine` transitions to `Cancelling` state but **never completes** because its body never ran - it was never dispatched

### Fix
The fix adds the same safety mechanism to `CoroutineStart.ATOMIC` that already existed for `CoroutineStart.DEFAULT`:

1. **`Cancellable.kt`**: Added `startCoroutineAtomic` function that wraps `startCoroutine` with `runSafely`. When the dispatcher throws during the initial dispatch:
   - The coroutine is completed with the exception (via `completion.resumeWith(Result.failure(...))`)
   - The exception is rethrown to the caller

2. **`CoroutineStart.kt`**: Changed `ATOMIC` to use `block.startCoroutineAtomic(receiver, completion)` instead of `block.startCoroutine(receiver, completion)`

This mirrors the existing pattern used by:
- `CoroutineStart.DEFAULT` â†’ `startCoroutineCancellable` â†’ `runSafely`/`dispatcherFailure`
- `CoroutineStart.UNDISPATCHED` â†’ `startCoroutineUndispatched` â†’ catches `DispatchException` and resumes completion

The `dispatcherFailure` function ensures that:
1. The coroutine job is properly completed (so it won't be a zombie preventing its parent from completion)
2. The exception is rethrown (so it's visible to the caller)

24â†’        actualCompletion.resumeWithException(reportException)

58â†’private inline fun <T> ScopeCoroutine<T>.undispatchedResult(
59â†’    shouldThrow: (Throwable) -> Boolean,
60â†’    startBlock: () -> Any?
61â†’): Any? {
62â†’    val result = try {
63â†’        startBlock()
64â†’    } catch (e: Throwable) {
65â†’        CompletedExceptionally(e)
66â†’    }
67â†’    /*
68â†’     * We're trying to complete our undispatched block here and have three code-paths:
69â†’     * (1) Coroutine is suspended.
70â†’     * Otherwise, coroutine had returned result, so we are completing our block (and its job).
71â†’     * (2) If we can't complete it or started waiting for children, we suspend.
72â†’     * (3) If we have successfully completed the coroutine state machine here,
73â†’     *     then we take the actual final state of the coroutine from makeCompletingOnce and return it.
74â†’     *
75â†’     * shouldThrow parameter is a special code path for timeout coroutine:
76â†’     * If timeout is exceeded, but withTimeout() block was not suspended, we would like to return block value,
77â†’     * not a timeout exception.
78â†’     */
79â†’    if (result === COROUTINE_SUSPENDED) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED // (1)
80â†’    val state = makeCompletingOnce(result)
81â†’    if (state === COMPLETING_WAITING_CHILDREN) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED // (2)
82â†’    afterCompletionUndispatched()
83â†’    return if (state is CompletedExceptionally) { // (3)
84â†’        when {
85â†’            shouldThrow(state.cause) -> throw recoverStackTrace(state.cause, uCont)
86â†’            result is CompletedExceptionally -> throw recoverStackTrace(result.cause, uCont)
87â†’            else -> result
88â†’        }
89â†’    } else {
90â†’        state.unboxState()
91â†’    }
92â†’}

 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * Buffers flow emissions via channel of a specified capacity and runs collector in a separate coroutine.
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * Normally, [flows][Flow] are _sequential_. It means that the code of all operators is executed in the
 18â†’ * same coroutine. For example, consider the following code using [onEach] and [collect] operators:
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * ```
 21â†’ * flowOf("A", "B", "C")
 22â†’ *     .onEach  { println("1$it") }
 23â†’ *     .collect { println("2$it") }
 24â†’ * ```
 25â†’ *
 26â†’ * It is going to be executed in the following order by the coroutine `Q` that calls this code:
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * ```
 29â†’ * Q : -->-- [1A] -- [2A] -- [1B] -- [2B] -- [1C] -- [2C] -->--
 30â†’ * ```
 31â†’ *
 32â†’ * So if the operator's code takes considerable time to execute, then the total execution time is going to be
 33â†’ * the sum of execution times for all operators.
 34â†’ *
 35â†’ * The `buffer` operator creates a separate coroutine during execution for the flow it applies to.
 36â†’ * Consider the following code:
 37â†’ *
 38â†’ * ```
 39â†’ * flowOf("A", "B", "C")
 40â†’ *     .onEach  { println("1$it") }
 41â†’ *     .buffer()  // <--------------- buffer between onEach and collect
 42â†’ *     .collect { println("2$it") }
 43â†’ * ```
 44â†’ *
 45â†’ * It will use two coroutines for execution of the code. A coroutine `Q` that calls this code is
 46â†’ * going to execute `collect`, and the code before `buffer` will be executed in a separate
 47â†’ * new coroutine `P` concurrently with `Q`:
 48â†’ *
 49â†’ * ```
 50â†’ * P : -->-- [1A] -- [1B] -- [1C] ---------->--  // flowOf(...).onEach { ... }
 51â†’ *
 52â†’ *                       |
 53â†’ *                       | channel               // buffer()
 54â†’ *                       V
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * Q : -->---------- [2A] -- [2B] -- [2C] -->--  // collect
 57â†’ * ```
 58â†’ *
 59â†’ * When the operator's code takes some time to execute, this decreases the total execution time of the flow.
 60â†’ * A [channel][Channel] is used between the coroutines to send elements emitted by the coroutine `P` to
 61â†’ * the coroutine `Q`. If the code before `buffer` operator (in the coroutine `P`) is faster than the code after
 62â†’ * `buffer` operator (in the coroutine `Q`), then this channel will become full at some point and will suspend
 63â†’ * the producer coroutine `P` until the consumer coroutine `Q` catches up.
 64â†’ * The [capacity] parameter defines the size of this buffer.
 65â†’ *
 66â†’ * ### Buffer overflow
 67â†’ *
 68â†’ * By default, the emitter is suspended when the buffer overflows, to let collector catch up. This strategy can be
 69â†’ * overridden with an optional [onBufferOverflow] parameter so that the emitter is never suspended. In this
 70â†’ * case, on buffer overflow either the oldest value in the buffer is dropped with the [DROP_OLDEST][BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST]
 71â†’ * strategy and the latest emitted value is added to the buffer,
 72â†’ * or the latest value that is being emitted is dropped with the [DROP_LATEST][BufferOverflow.DROP_LATEST] strategy,
 73â†’ * keeping the buffer intact.
 74â†’ * To implement either of the custom strategies, a buffer of at least one element is used.
 75â†’ *
 76â†’ * ### Operator fusion
 77â†’ *
 78â†’ * Adjacent applications of [channelFlow], [flowOn], [buffer], and [produceIn] are
 79â†’ * always fused so that only one properly configured channel is used for execution.
 80â†’ *
 81â†’ * Explicitly specified buffer capacity takes precedence over `buffer()` or `buffer(Channel.BUFFERED)` calls,
 82â†’ * which effectively requests a buffer of any size. Multiple requests with a specified buffer
 83â†’ * size produce a buffer with the sum of the requested buffer sizes.
 84â†’ *
 85â†’ * A `buffer` call with a non-default value of the [onBufferOverflow] parameter overrides all immediately preceding
 86â†’ * buffering operators, because it never suspends its upstream, and thus no upstream buffer would ever be used.
 87â†’ *
 88â†’ * ### Conceptual implementation
 89â†’ *
 90â†’ * The actual implementation of `buffer` is not trivial due to the fusing, but conceptually its basic
 91â†’ * implementation is equivalent to the following code that can be written using [produce]
 92â†’ * coroutine builder to produce a channel and [consumeEach][ReceiveChannel.consumeEach] extension to consume it:
 93â†’ *
 94â†’ * ```
 95â†’ * fun <T> Flow<T>.buffer(capacity: Int = DEFAULT): Flow<T> = flow {
 96â†’ *     coroutineScope { // limit the scope of concurrent producer coroutine
 97â†’ *         val channel = produce(capacity = capacity) {
 98â†’ *             collect { send(it) } // send all to channel
 99â†’ *         }
100â†’ *         // emit all received values
101â†’ *         channel.consumeEach { emit(it) }
102â†’ *     }
103â†’ * }
104â†’ * ```
105â†’ *
106â†’ * ### Conflation
107â†’ *
108â†’ * Usage of this function with [capacity] of [Channel.CONFLATED][Channel.CONFLATED] is a shortcut to
109â†’ * `buffer(onBufferOverflow = `[`BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST`][BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST]`)`, and is available via
110â†’ * a separate [conflate] operator. See its documentation for details.
111â†’ *
112â†’ * @param capacity type/capacity of the buffer between coroutines. Allowed values are the same as in `Channel(...)`
113â†’ *   factory function: [BUFFERED][Channel.BUFFERED] (by default), [CONFLATED][Channel.CONFLATED],
114â†’ *   [RENDEZVOUS][Channel.RENDEZVOUS], [UNLIMITED][Channel.UNLIMITED] or a non-negative value indicating
115â†’ *   an explicitly requested size.
116â†’ * @param onBufferOverflow configures an action on buffer overflow (optional, defaults to
117â†’ *   [SUSPEND][BufferOverflow.SUSPEND], supported only when `capacity >= 0` or `capacity == Channel.BUFFERED`,
118â†’ *   implicitly creates a channel with at least one buffered element).
119â†’ */
120â†’@Suppress("NAME_SHADOWING")
121â†’public fun <T> Flow<T>.buffer(capacity: Int = BUFFERED, onBufferOverflow: BufferOverflow = BufferOverflow.SUSPEND): Flow<T> {
122â†’    require(capacity >= 0 || capacity == BUFFERED || capacity == CONFLATED) {
123â†’        "Buffer size should be non-negative, BUFFERED, or CONFLATED, but was $capacity"
124â†’    }
125â†’    require(capacity != CONFLATED || onBufferOverflow == BufferOverflow.SUSPEND) {
126â†’        "CONFLATED capacity cannot be used with non-default onBufferOverflow"
127â†’    }
128â†’    // desugar CONFLATED capacity to (0, DROP_OLDEST)
129â†’    var capacity = capacity
130â†’    var onBufferOverflow = onBufferOverflow
131â†’    if (capacity == CONFLATED) {
132â†’        capacity = 0
133â†’        onBufferOverflow = BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST
134â†’    }
135â†’    // create a flow
136â†’    return when (this) {
137â†’        is FusibleFlow -> fuse(capacity = capacity, onBufferOverflow = onBufferOverflow)
138â†’        else -> ChannelFlowOperatorImpl(this, capacity = capacity, onBufferOverflow = onBufferOverflow)
139â†’    }
140â†’}
141â†’
142â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.4.0, binary compatibility with earlier versions")
143â†’public fun <T> Flow<T>.buffer(capacity: Int = BUFFERED): Flow<T> = buffer(capacity)

145â†’/**
146â†’ * Conflates flow emissions via conflated channel and runs collector in a separate coroutine.
147â†’ * The effect of this is that emitter is never suspended due to a slow collector, but collector
148â†’ * always gets the most recent value emitted.
149â†’ *
150â†’ * For example, consider the flow that emits integers from 1 to 30 with 100 ms delay between them:
151â†’ *
152â†’ * ```
153â†’ * val flow = flow {
154â†’ *     for (i in 1..30) {
155â†’ *         delay(100)
156â†’ *         emit(i)
157â†’ *     }
158â†’ * }
159â†’ * ```
160â†’ *
161â†’ * Applying `conflate()` operator to it allows a collector that delays 1 second on each element to get
162â†’ * integers 1, 10, 20, 30:
163â†’ *
164â†’ * ```
165â†’ * val result = flow.conflate().onEach { delay(1000) }.toList()
166â†’ * assertEquals(listOf(1, 10, 20, 30), result)
167â†’ * ```
168â†’ *
169â†’ * Note that `conflate` operator is a shortcut for [buffer] with `capacity` of [Channel.CONFLATED][Channel.CONFLATED],
170â†’ * which is, in turn, a shortcut to a buffer that only keeps the latest element as
171â†’ * created by `buffer(onBufferOverflow = `[`BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST`][BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST]`)`.
172â†’ *
173â†’ * ### Operator fusion
174â†’ *
175â†’ * Adjacent applications of `conflate`/[buffer], [channelFlow], [flowOn] and [produceIn] are
176â†’ * always fused so that only one properly configured channel is used for execution.
177â†’ * **Conflation takes precedence over `buffer()` calls with any other capacity.**
178â†’ *
179â†’ * Note that any instance of [StateFlow] already behaves as if `conflate` operator is
180â†’ * applied to it, so applying `conflate` to a `StateFlow` has no effect.
181â†’ * See [StateFlow] documentation on Operator Fusion.
182â†’ */
183â†’public fun <T> Flow<T>.conflate(): Flow<T> = buffer(CONFLATED)

185â†’/**
186â†’ * Changes the context where this flow is executed to the given [context].
187â†’ * This operator is composable and affects only preceding operators that do not have its own context.
188â†’ * This operator is context preserving: [context] **does not** leak into the downstream flow.

194â†’ *     val singleValue = intFlow // will be executed on IO if context wasn't specified before
195â†’ *         .map { ... } // Will be executed in IO
196â†’ *         .flowOn(Dispatchers.IO)
197â†’ *         .filter { ... } // Will be executed in Default
198â†’ *         .flowOn(Dispatchers.Default)
199â†’ *         .single() // Will be executed in the Main
200â†’ * }
201â†’ * ```
202â†’ *
203â†’ * For more explanation of context preservation please refer to [Flow] documentation.
204â†’ *
205â†’ * This operator retains a _sequential_ nature of flow if changing the context does not call for changing
206â†’ * the [dispatcher][CoroutineDispatcher]. Otherwise, if changing dispatcher is required, it collects
207â†’ * flow emissions in one coroutine that is run using a specified [context] and emits them from another coroutines
208â†’ * with the original collector's context using a channel with a [default][Channel.BUFFERED] buffer size
209â†’ * between two coroutines similarly to [buffer] operator, unless [buffer] operator is explicitly called
210â†’ * before or after `flowOn`, which requests buffering behavior and specifies channel size.
211â†’ *

213â†’ * In particular, this operator ensures that downstream flow does not resume on cancellation even if the element
214â†’ * was already emitted by the upstream flow.
215â†’ *
216â†’ * ### Operator fusion
217â†’ *
218â†’ * Adjacent applications of [channelFlow], [flowOn], [buffer], and [produceIn] are
219â†’ * always fused so that only one properly configured channel is used for execution.
220â†’ *

 17â†’ * - [DEFAULT] immediately schedules the coroutine for execution according to its context.
 18â†’ * - [LAZY] delays the moment of the initial dispatch until the result of the coroutine is needed.
 19â†’ * - [ATOMIC] prevents the coroutine from being cancelled before it starts, ensuring that its code will start

 37â†’     *     println("1. About to start a new coroutine.")

 40â†’     *     // In this case, it's the single thread backing `runBlocking`.

 47â†’     *

55â†’     * dispatcher implementation or missing Dispatchers.Main.
